issues: The title is a bit long and slightly list-like; it can be tightened for readability.
The 'likelySearchQuery' values read like internal search notes and should be removed or clearly relabeled if this page is public.
The screenshot finding for the Users screen is phrased as an interpretation; keep it clearly tied to the visible mismatch rather than implying a confirmed hidden account from that screenshot alone.

The proofStatement mentions 'collected during real WordPress cleanup work by MD Pabel'; that is acceptable as background, but if this page is public it may read stronger than needed and could be softened to avoid implying independent certification.
Root cause is appropriately low/unknown and should remain that way; do not promote it in the page copy.
fixes: Shorten the title to something like: 'WordPress MU-Plugin Backdoor Hiding an Admin User'.
Remove the 'likelySearchQuery' field from the published page or move it to internal notes only.
Reword the Users screen caption to say the counts are 'consistent with user-query tampering' rather than stating a hidden admin is confirmed from the screenshot alone.

If you want a more neutral proof statement, use: 'Based on representative malware samples and screenshots from a real WordPress cleanup case, this pattern shows malicious code in wp-content/mu-plugins that enables gated remote PHP execution and hides a selected WordPress user from admin listings.'
Keep the root-cause section explicitly marked unknown/low confidence and avoid cause-and-effect claims about the initial entry path beyond what the evidence supports.
